comment_text.sn12.51:3.1: Pali is rich in expressions for inner reflection and investigation. Sometimes this process is called “inner self-examination” (SN 12.66:3.3, sometimes “rational application of mind” (SN 12.4:2.3).
comment_text.sn12.51:9.1: This is one of the passages showing that the primary sense of saṅkhāra is moral “choices” either good, bad, or “imperturbable”. See also MN 57:9.2, AN 3.23, AN 4.233. | The suffix -upaga indicates “passing on” to rebirth in a new life. The compound puññūpaga parallels kammupaga and means “passes on by means of goodness” (resolve to puññena upagataṁ).
comment_text.sn12.51:9.3: The fourth absorption and above are called “imperturbable” (āneñja) because they are associated with equanimous feeling. This is technically distinguished from “good” or “meritorious” (puñña) choices, whose outcome is pleasant feeling.
comment_text.sn12.51:9.4: This shows that the sense of “choices” here is morally pregnant: they make no choice productive of future lives. They still make “choices” which are in line with the good, they just don’t have any kammic effect.
comment_text.sn12.51:9.6: Letting go is not just a conceptual matter, it has immediate emotional consequences. Paritassati conveys the twin senses of desire and agitation, for which “anxiety” seems the best fit.
comment_text.sn12.51:10.1: This describes the reflective experience of the arahant.
comment_text.sn12.51:11.2: To “become cool” is, as the simile makes clear, to realize extinguishment (nibbāna).

root_text.sn12.51:1.2: ekaṁ samayaṁ bhagavā sāvatthiyaṁ viharati jetavane anāthapiṇḍikassa ārāme.
root_text.sn12.51:2.1: “Kittāvatā nu kho, bhikkhave, bhikkhu parivīmaṁsamāno parivīmaṁseyya sabbaso sammā dukkhakkhayāyā”ti?
root_text.sn12.51:2.2: “Bhagavaṁmūlakā no, bhante, dhammā bhagavaṁnettikā bhagavaṁpaṭisaraṇā. Sādhu vata, bhante, bhagavantaṁyeva paṭibhātu etassa bhāsitassa attho. Bhagavato sutvā bhikkhū dhāressantī”ti.
root_text.sn12.51:2.3: “Tena hi, bhikkhave, suṇātha, sādhukaṁ manasi karotha, bhāsissāmī”ti.
root_text.sn12.51:2.4: “Evaṁ, bhante”ti kho te bhikkhū bhagavato paccassosuṁ.
root_text.sn12.51:3.2: ‘yaṁ kho idaṁ anekavidhaṁ nānappakārakaṁ dukkhaṁ loke uppajjati jarāmaraṇaṁ;
root_text.sn12.51:3.3: idaṁ nu kho dukkhaṁ kiṁnidānaṁ kiṁsamudayaṁ kiṁjātikaṁ kiṁpabhavaṁ?
root_text.sn12.51:3.4: Kismiṁ sati jarāmaraṇaṁ hoti, kismiṁ asati jarāmaraṇaṁ na hotī’ti?
root_text.sn12.51:3.6: ‘yaṁ kho idaṁ anekavidhaṁ nānappakārakaṁ dukkhaṁ loke uppajjati jarāmaraṇaṁ, idaṁ kho dukkhaṁ jātinidānaṁ jātisamudayaṁ jātijātikaṁ jātippabhavaṁ.
root_text.sn12.51:3.7: Jātiyā sati jarāmaraṇaṁ hoti, jātiyā asati jarāmaraṇaṁ na hotī’ti.
root_text.sn12.51:4.1: So jarāmaraṇañca pajānāti, jarāmaraṇasamudayañca pajānāti, jarāmaraṇanirodhañca pajānāti, yā ca jarāmaraṇanirodhasāruppagāminī paṭipadā tañca pajānāti, tathā paṭipanno ca hoti anudhammacārī;
root_text.sn12.51:4.2: ayaṁ vuccati, bhikkhave, bhikkhu sabbaso sammā dukkhakkhayāya paṭipanno jarāmaraṇanirodhāya.
root_text.sn12.51:5.2: ‘jāti panāyaṁ kiṁnidānā kiṁsamudayā kiṁjātikā kiṁpabhavā, kismiṁ sati jāti hoti, kismiṁ asati jāti na hotī’ti?
root_text.sn12.51:5.5: bhave sati jāti hoti, bhave asati jāti na hotī’ti.
root_text.sn12.51:6.1: So jātiñca pajānāti, jātisamudayañca pajānāti, jātinirodhañca pajānāti, yā ca jātinirodhasāruppagāminī paṭipadā tañca pajānāti, tathā paṭipanno ca hoti anudhammacārī;
root_text.sn12.51:6.2: ayaṁ vuccati, bhikkhave, bhikkhu sabbaso sammā dukkhakkhayāya paṭipanno jātinirodhāya.
root_text.sn12.51:7.10: saṅkhārā panime kiṁnidānā kiṁsamudayā kiṁjātikā kiṁpabhavā;
root_text.sn12.51:7.11: kismiṁ sati saṅkhārā honti, kismiṁ asati saṅkhārā na hontī’ti?
root_text.sn12.51:7.14: avijjāya sati saṅkhārā honti, avijjāya asati saṅkhārā na hontī’ti.
root_text.sn12.51:8.1: So saṅkhāre ca pajānāti, saṅkhārasamudayañca pajānāti, saṅkhāranirodhañca pajānāti, yā ca saṅkhāranirodhasāruppagāminī paṭipadā tañca pajānāti, tathā paṭipanno ca hoti anudhammacārī;
root_text.sn12.51:8.2: ayaṁ vuccati, bhikkhave, bhikkhu sabbaso sammā dukkhakkhayāya paṭipanno saṅkhāranirodhāya.
root_text.sn12.51:9.1: Avijjāgato yaṁ, bhikkhave, purisapuggalo puññañce saṅkhāraṁ abhisaṅkharoti, puññūpagaṁ hoti viññāṇaṁ.
root_text.sn12.51:9.2: Apuññañce saṅkhāraṁ abhisaṅkharoti, apuññūpagaṁ hoti viññāṇaṁ.
root_text.sn12.51:9.3: Āneñjañce saṅkhāraṁ abhisaṅkharoti āneñjūpagaṁ hoti viññāṇaṁ.
root_text.sn12.51:9.4: Yato kho, bhikkhave, bhikkhuno avijjā pahīnā hoti vijjā uppannā, so avijjāvirāgā vijjuppādā neva puññābhisaṅkhāraṁ abhisaṅkharoti na apuññābhisaṅkhāraṁ abhisaṅkharoti na āneñjābhisaṅkhāraṁ abhisaṅkharoti.
root_text.sn12.51:9.5: Anabhisaṅkharonto anabhisañcetayanto na kiñci loke upādiyati;
root_text.sn12.51:9.6: anupādiyaṁ na paritassati, aparitassaṁ paccattaññeva parinibbāyati.
root_text.sn12.51:9.7: ‘Khīṇā jāti, vusitaṁ brahmacariyaṁ, kataṁ karaṇīyaṁ, nāparaṁ itthattāyā’ti pajānāti.
root_text.sn12.51:10.1: So sukhañce vedanaṁ vedayati, sā aniccāti pajānāti, anajjhositāti pajānāti, anabhinanditāti pajānāti.
root_text.sn12.51:10.2: Dukkhañce vedanaṁ vedayati, sā aniccāti pajānāti, anajjhositāti pajānāti, anabhinanditāti pajānāti.
root_text.sn12.51:10.3: Adukkhamasukhañce vedanaṁ vedayati, sā aniccāti pajānāti, anajjhositāti pajānāti, anabhinanditāti pajānāti.
root_text.sn12.51:10.4: So sukhañce vedanaṁ vedayati, visaṁyutto naṁ vedayati.
root_text.sn12.51:10.5: Dukkhañce vedanaṁ vedayati, visaṁyutto naṁ vedayati.
root_text.sn12.51:10.6: Adukkhamasukhañce vedanaṁ vedayati, visaṁyutto naṁ vedayati.
root_text.sn12.51:11.1: So kāyapariyantikaṁ vedanaṁ vedayamāno kāyapariyantikaṁ vedanaṁ vedayāmīti pajānāti, jīvitapariyantikaṁ vedanaṁ vedayamāno jīvitapariyantikaṁ vedanaṁ vedayāmīti pajānāti.
root_text.sn12.51:11.2: Kāyassa bhedā uddhaṁ jīvitapariyādānā idheva sabbavedayitāni anabhinanditāni sītībhavissanti, sarīrāni avasissantīti pajānāti.
root_text.sn12.51:12.1: Seyyathāpi, bhikkhave, puriso kumbhakārapākā uṇhaṁ kumbhaṁ uddharitvā same bhūmibhāge paṭisisseyya.
root_text.sn12.51:12.2: Tatra yāyaṁ usmā sā tattheva vūpasameyya, kapallāni avasisseyyuṁ.
root_text.sn12.51:12.3: Evameva kho, bhikkhave, bhikkhu kāyapariyantikaṁ vedanaṁ vedayamāno kāyapariyantikaṁ vedanaṁ vedayāmīti pajānāti, jīvitapariyantikaṁ vedanaṁ vedayamāno jīvitapariyantikaṁ vedanaṁ vedayāmīti pajānāti.
root_text.sn12.51:12.4: Kāyassa bhedā uddhaṁ jīvitapariyādānā idheva sabbavedayitāni anabhinanditāni sītībhavissanti, sarīrāni avasissantīti pajānāti.
root_text.sn12.51:13.2: api nu kho khīṇāsavo bhikkhu puññābhisaṅkhāraṁ vā abhisaṅkhareyya apuññābhisaṅkhāraṁ vā abhisaṅkhareyya āneñjābhisaṅkhāraṁ vā abhisaṅkhareyyā”ti?
root_text.sn12.51:14.1: “Sabbaso vā pana saṅkhāresu asati, saṅkhāranirodhā api nu kho viññāṇaṁ paññāyethā”ti?
root_text.sn12.51:15.1: “Sabbaso vā pana viññāṇe asati, viññāṇanirodhā api nu kho nāmarūpaṁ paññāyethā”ti?
root_text.sn12.51:16.1: “Sabbaso vā pana nāmarūpe asati, nāmarūpanirodhā api nu kho saḷāyatanaṁ paññāyethā”ti?
root_text.sn12.51:17.1: “Sabbaso vā pana saḷāyatane asati, saḷāyatananirodhā api nu kho phasso paññāyethā”ti?
root_text.sn12.51:18.1: “Sabbaso vā pana phasse asati, phassanirodhā api nu kho vedanā paññāyethā”ti?
root_text.sn12.51:19.1: “Sabbaso vā pana vedanāya asati, vedanānirodhā api nu kho taṇhā paññāyethā”ti?
root_text.sn12.51:20.1: “Sabbaso vā pana taṇhāya asati, taṇhānirodhā api nu kho upādānaṁ paññāyethā”ti?
root_text.sn12.51:21.1: “Sabbaso vā pana upādāne asati, upādānanirodhā api nu kho bhavo paññāyethā”ti.
root_text.sn12.51:22.1: “Sabbaso vā pana bhave asati, bhavanirodhā api nu kho jāti paññāyethā”ti?
root_text.sn12.51:23.1: “Sabbaso vā pana jātiyā asati, jātinirodhā api nu kho jarāmaraṇaṁ paññāyethā”ti?
root_text.sn12.51:24.1: “Sādhu sādhu, bhikkhave, evametaṁ, bhikkhave, netaṁ aññathā.
root_text.sn12.51:24.2: Saddahatha me taṁ, bhikkhave, adhimuccatha, nikkaṅkhā ettha hotha nibbicikicchā.

translation_text.sn12.51:1.2: At one time the Buddha was staying near Sāvatthī in Jeta’s Grove, Anāthapiṇḍika’s monastery.
translation_text.sn12.51:1.3: There the Buddha addressed the mendicants,
translation_text.sn12.51:2.1: “Mendicants, when a mendicant is inquiring, how do you define when they are inquiring for the complete ending of suffering?”
translation_text.sn12.51:2.2: “Our teachings are rooted in the Buddha. He is our guide and our refuge. Sir, may the Buddha himself please clarify the meaning of this. The mendicants will listen and remember it.”
translation_text.sn12.51:2.3: “Well then, mendicants, listen and apply your mind well, I will speak.”
translation_text.sn12.51:3.1: “Mendicants, take a mendicant who makes an inquiry:
translation_text.sn12.51:3.2: ‘The suffering that arises in the world starting with old age and death takes many and diverse forms.
translation_text.sn12.51:3.3: What is the source, origin, birthplace, and inception of this suffering?
translation_text.sn12.51:3.4: When what exists do old age and death come to be? When what does not exist do old age and death not come to be?’
translation_text.sn12.51:3.5: While making an inquiry they understand:
translation_text.sn12.51:3.6: ‘The suffering that arises in the world starting with old age and death takes many and diverse forms. The source of this suffering is rebirth.
translation_text.sn12.51:3.7: When rebirth exists, old age and death come to be. When rebirth doesn’t exist, old age and death don’t come to be.’
translation_text.sn12.51:4.1: They understand old age and death, their origin, their cessation, and the fitting practice for their cessation. And they practice in line with that path.
translation_text.sn12.51:4.2: This is called a mendicant who is practicing for the complete ending of suffering, for the cessation of old age and death.
translation_text.sn12.51:5.2: ‘But what is the source of this rebirth? When what exists does rebirth come to be? And when what does not exist does rebirth not come to be?’
translation_text.sn12.51:5.3: While making an inquiry they understand:
translation_text.sn12.51:5.4: ‘Continued existence is the source of rebirth.
translation_text.sn12.51:5.5: When continued existence exists, rebirth comes to be. When continued existence does not exist, rebirth doesn’t come to be.’
translation_text.sn12.51:6.1: They understand rebirth, its origin, its cessation, and the fitting practice for its cessation. And they practice in line with that path.
translation_text.sn12.51:6.2: This is called a mendicant who is practicing for the complete ending of suffering, for the cessation of rebirth.
translation_text.sn12.51:7.2: ‘But what is the source of this continued existence? …’ …
translation_text.sn12.51:7.3: ‘But what is the source of this grasping? …’ …
translation_text.sn12.51:7.4: ‘But what is the source of this craving? …’ …
translation_text.sn12.51:7.5: ‘But what is the source of this feeling? …’ …
translation_text.sn12.51:7.6: ‘But what is the source of this contact? …’ …
translation_text.sn12.51:7.7: ‘But what is the source of these six sense fields? …’ …
translation_text.sn12.51:7.8: ‘But what is the source of this name and form? …’ …
translation_text.sn12.51:7.9: ‘But what is the source of this consciousness? …’ …
translation_text.sn12.51:7.10: ‘But what is the source of these choices?
translation_text.sn12.51:7.11: When what exists do choices come to be? When what does not exist do choices not come to be?’
translation_text.sn12.51:7.12: While making an inquiry they understand:
translation_text.sn12.51:7.14: When ignorance exists, choices come to be. When ignorance does not exist, choices don’t come to be.’
translation_text.sn12.51:8.1: They understand choices, their origin, their cessation, and the fitting practice for their cessation. And they practice in line with that path.
translation_text.sn12.51:8.2: This is called a mendicant who is practicing for the complete ending of suffering, for the cessation of choices.
translation_text.sn12.51:9.1: If an ignorant individual makes a good choice, consciousness passes on by means of goodness.
translation_text.sn12.51:9.2: If they make a bad choice, consciousness passes on by means of badness.
translation_text.sn12.51:9.3: If they make an imperturbable choice, consciousness passes on by means of imperturbability.
translation_text.sn12.51:9.4: When a mendicant has given up ignorance and given rise to knowledge, with the fading away of ignorance and the arising of knowledge they don’t make a good choice, a bad choice, or an imperturbable choice.
translation_text.sn12.51:9.5: Not choosing or intending, they don’t grasp at anything in the world.
translation_text.sn12.51:9.6: Not grasping, they’re not anxious. Not being anxious, they personally become extinguished.
translation_text.sn12.51:9.7: They understand: ‘Rebirth is ended, the spiritual journey has been completed, what had to be done has been done, there is nothing further for this place.’
translation_text.sn12.51:10.1: If they feel a pleasant feeling, they understand that it’s impermanent, that they’re not attached to it, and that they don’t take pleasure in it.
translation_text.sn12.51:10.2: If they feel a painful feeling, they understand that it’s impermanent, that they’re not attached to it, and that they don’t take pleasure in it.
translation_text.sn12.51:10.3: If they feel a neutral feeling, they understand that it’s impermanent, that they’re not attached to it, and that they don’t take pleasure in it.
translation_text.sn12.51:10.4: If they feel a pleasant feeling, they feel it detached.
translation_text.sn12.51:10.5: If they feel a painful feeling, they feel it detached.
translation_text.sn12.51:10.6: If they feel a neutral feeling, they feel it detached.
translation_text.sn12.51:11.1: Feeling the end of the body draw close, they understand: ‘I feel the end of the body draw close.’ Feeling the end of life draw close, they understand: ‘I feel the end of life draw close.’
translation_text.sn12.51:11.2: They understand: ‘When my body breaks up and my life has come to an end, everything that’s felt, since I no longer take pleasure in it, will become cool right here. Only bodily remains will be left.’
translation_text.sn12.51:12.1: Suppose a person were to remove a hot clay pot from a potter’s kiln and place it down on level ground.
translation_text.sn12.51:12.2: Its heat would dissipate right there, and the shards would be left behind.
translation_text.sn12.51:12.3: In the same way, feeling the end of the body draw close, they understand: ‘I feel the end of the body draw close.’ Feeling the end of life draw close, they understand: ‘I feel the end of life draw close.’
translation_text.sn12.51:12.4: They understand: ‘When my body breaks up and my life has come to an end, everything that’s felt, since I no longer take pleasure in it, will become cool right here. Only bodily remains will be left.’
translation_text.sn12.51:13.2: Would a mendicant who has ended the defilements still make good choices, bad choices, or imperturbable choices?”
translation_text.sn12.51:14.1: “And when there are no choices at all, with the cessation of choices, would consciousness still be found?”
translation_text.sn12.51:15.1: “And when there’s no consciousness at all, would name and form still be found?”
translation_text.sn12.51:16.1: “And when there are no name and form at all, would the six sense fields still be found?”
translation_text.sn12.51:17.1: “And when there are no six sense fields at all, would contact still be found?”
translation_text.sn12.51:18.1: “And when there’s no contact at all, would feeling still be found?”
translation_text.sn12.51:19.1: “And when there’s no feeling at all, would craving still be found?”
translation_text.sn12.51:20.1: “And when there’s no craving at all, would grasping still be found?”
translation_text.sn12.51:21.1: “And when there’s no grasping at all, would continued existence still be found?”
translation_text.sn12.51:22.1: “And when there’s no continued existence at all, would rebirth still be found?”
translation_text.sn12.51:23.1: “And when there’s no rebirth at all, would old age and death still be found?”
translation_text.sn12.51:24.1: “Good, good, mendicants! That’s how it is, not otherwise.
translation_text.sn12.51:24.2: Trust me on this, mendicants; be convinced. Have no doubts or uncertainties in this matter.
translation_text.sn12.51:24.3: Just this is the end of suffering.”

variant_text.sn12.51:10.4: visaṁyutto naṁ → taṁ vedanaṁ (bj, pts1ed, pts2ed); vedanaṁ (mr)
variant_text.sn12.51:12.1: paṭisisseyya → paṭiviseyya (bj); patiṭṭhapeyya (sya-all, km, pts1ed, pts2ed); paṭiseveyya (ṭīkā)
